questions.prompt: Under accrual basis accounting, when is revenue recognised?
choices: When an invoice is sent to the customer
When the service is performed or goods are delivered
questions.explanation: Accrual basis recognises revenue when EARNED — when the service is performed or goods are delivered — regardless of when cash is received. This matches revenue to the period it was earned.

questions.prompt: A consulting firm completes a $10,000 project in December but receives payment in January. Under accrual accounting, in which month is revenue recognised?
choices: December — when the service is performed
Split evenly between December and January
Neither — only when the invoice is issued
questions.explanation: Revenue is recognised in December when EARNED (work completed), not when cash arrives in January. Accrual basis separates the timing of earning revenue from receiving payment.

questions.prompt: A company pays $24,000 for 12 months of insurance on 1 October. By 31 December, what amount should be recorded as an expense?
questions.explanation: From October 1 to December 31 = 3 months used. Expense = $24,000 ÷ 12 × 3 = $6,000. The remaining $18,000 stays as Prepaid Insurance (an asset — future benefit not yet consumed).

questions.prompt: Employees earn $15,000 in December salaries but will be paid in January. In December, which types of accounts are affected and how? (Activity 2 style)
questions.explanation: Salary Expense increases $15,000 (expense incurred → reduces equity). Salaries Payable (Liability) increases $15,000 (obligation to pay in January). No cash has moved yet — cash only decreases when payment is made in January.

questions.prompt: A customer pays $6,000 on 1 November for 3 months of service (Nov–Jan). On 30 November, one month of service is earned. Which accounts are affected?
questions.explanation: When the service is performed, Unearned Revenue (Liability) decreases $2,000 — the obligation is fulfilled. Revenue increases $2,000 — now actually earned. The other $4,000 remains as Unearned Revenue (Liability) for Nov–Jan remaining.

questions.prompt: What does the matching principle require?
choices: Revenue must equal expenses each period
All accounts must balance to zero at year end
Expenses must be recorded in the same period as the revenue they helped generate
All transactions must have two equal entries
questions.explanation: The matching principle ensures expenses are recorded in the same period as the revenue they helped produce. This gives an accurate profit figure — not distorted by timing of cash payments.

questions.prompt: A company performs $8,000 of consulting work in December but has not yet collected payment. Which accounts are affected and how?
questions.explanation: Revenue is earned in December even though cash hasn't been received. Accounts Receivable (Asset) increases $8,000 — money owed by the client. Revenue increases $8,000 — earned this period. This ensures revenue is matched to December where it was earned.

questions.prompt: Unearned revenue is best classified as:
choices: An asset — it represents future cash to be received
A liability — the company owes a future service to the customer
Equity — the customer has invested in the company
An expense — the company has incurred future costs
questions.explanation: Unearned Revenue is a LIABILITY because the company has received cash but has NOT yet performed the service. It owes the customer a future service — that is an obligation. Once the service is performed, Unearned Revenue (Liability) decreases and Revenue increases.

questions.prompt: On 1 September, a company pays $3,600 for 6 months of rent (Sept–Feb). By 31 December, what amount REMAINS as Prepaid Rent?
questions.explanation: $3,600 ÷ 6 months = $600 per month. Sept to Dec = 4 months used → Rent Expense = $2,400. Remaining Prepaid (Jan–Feb = 2 months) = 2 × $600 = $1,200. The question asks what REMAINS as an asset, which is $1,200.

questions.prompt: Mary hired a new employee at a monthly salary of $3,000. The employee will start work on 1 April. What is the effect on Mary's accounts today? (Activity 2, Q1 v)
choices: No effect — no transaction has occurred yet
questions.explanation: Hiring an employee is not a transaction until work is performed and compensation is owed. Since the employee hasn't started yet, there is NO financial effect today. Expenses are only recorded when they are incurred — not when a future commitment is made.

questions.prompt: On 1 Nov, a company receives $9,600 for a 12-month maintenance contract (Nov to Oct). At 31 Dec (year end), 2 months have been earned. Which accounts are affected for the adjustment?
questions.explanation: Monthly rate = $9,600 ÷ 12 = $800. Nov + Dec = 2 months earned = $1,600. Unearned Revenue (Liability) decreases $1,600 — the obligation for 2 months is now fulfilled. Revenue increases $1,600 — now earned. Remaining $8,000 stays as Unearned Revenue (Liability) for the next 10 months.

questions.prompt: Henry's firm received $2,000 cash on 29 Jan for consulting to be done in February, and earned $3,000 on credit on 24 Jan (not yet collected). Under accrual accounting, how much revenue is recognised in January? (Activity 2, Q2)
choices: $5,000 — both cash received and credit service
questions.explanation: Under accrual accounting: the $2,000 received in advance is NOT revenue yet — it is Unearned Revenue (Liability) because the service is in February. The $3,000 credit service IS revenue in January because it was earned in January (service already performed). Accrual revenue = $3,000 only. Cash basis would record $2,000 (cash received) and ignore the $3,000 not yet collected.
